did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used both a deep learning method (Convolutional Neural Network based on pre-trained Inception-v3) and a classical machine learning method (ensemble boosted tree classifier with homology segmentation) to automatically classify benign vs. malignant dermoscopic skin lesion images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work where the authors propose and compare two AI models (a classical machine learning method and a deep learning method) for classifying benign vs. malignant dermoscopic images. It includes specific methodology sections (Dataset, Machine Learning Approach, Deep Learning Approach), reports original performance metrics (accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, precision) on a disjoint test set, and concludes with new findings regarding the superior generalization ability of the deep learning model.

title: Artificial Intelligence Algorithms for Benign vs. Malignant Dermoscopic Skin Lesion Image Classification
brief_summary: This study aimed to improve early melanoma diagnosis by comparing a classical machine learning method with a deep learning approach for classifying benign versus malignant dermoscopic skin lesion images. The researchers utilized a combined training dataset of 25,122 images sourced from the ISIC 2019 challenge, a publicly available dataset, and the Pedro Hispano Hospital dataset, which was balanced into ten subsets to address class imbalance. For evaluation, both models were tested on a disjoint test set of 200 images to assess generalization capabilities. The deep learning model, based on a pre-trained Inception-v3 convolutional neural network, achieved an accuracy of 85.4 ± 3.2% and specificity of 75.5 ± 7.6%. In contrast, the machine learning model, which employed an ensemble boosted tree classifier with homology segmentation, yielded lower performance with 73.8 ± 1.1% accuracy and 44.5 ± 4.7% specificity. The results demonstrated that the deep learning method outperformed the machine learning approach, showing superior generalization ability for automated skin lesion classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classes used in the benign vs. malignant classification task. The dataset included benign images from the Pedro Hispano Hospital dataset and the ISIC 2019 challenge, balanced with malignant images.

One of the two binary classes used in the benign vs. malignant classification task. The study focuses on early melanoma diagnosis, and the malignant class primarily consists of melanoma, though the binary label 'malignant' was used for the classification model.

The primary clinical focus of the study for early diagnosis. The binary 'malignant' class in the experiment corresponds to melanoma cases, as indicated by the title and abstract context regarding melanoma incidence and diagnosis.

Mentioned in the context of comparing the study's results with other works (López-Labraca et al., Xie et al., He et al.) that performed 'seborrheic keratosis vs. all' classification. Not a target class in this study's binary benign vs. malignant experiment.

Mentioned in the context of comparing the study's results with other works (López-Labraca et al., Xie et al., He et al.) that used datasets formed by common nevi, melanoma, and seborrheic keratosis. Not a distinct target class in this study's binary experiment.

all_task_types.target: Benign vs. Malignant dermoscopic skin lesion images
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task to differentiate between benign and malignant skin lesions using two models: a classical machine learning method (ensemble boosted tree classifier with homology segmentation) and a deep learning method (pre-trained Inception-v3 CNN). The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (benign or malignant) based on dermoscopic images.

datasets_used.name: DB1 (ISIC 2019 Challenge)
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermoscopic image dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2019 challenge. Images with pen marks and colored patches were removed to avoid confounding factors.

use.training: Combined with DB2 to form the training dataset. Ten balanced subsets of 19,932 images (65% of benign images randomly selected) were created for training and validation.
use.validation: Used for validation within the 10-fold cross-validation scheme on the combined training set.

datasets_used.name: DB2 (Open Dataset)
datasets_used.description: An open dataset available upon request [18]. It does not provide age information.

use.training: Combined with DB1 to form the total training dataset of 25,122 images.

datasets_used.name: DB3 (Pedro Hispano Hospital)
datasets_used.description: A dataset provided by Pedro Hispano Hospital (PH2). It provides neither age nor sex information.

use.testing: Used as a disjoint test set to evaluate the generalization capability of both the deep learning and machine learning models.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To train and validate the models by creating 10 different, randomly selected balanced datasets from the training set and averaging the performance metrics.
To report the average performance and variability of accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, and precision across the 10 validation/test operations.
To evaluate the failure rate of the preprocessing and segmentation algorithm on the training dataset images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the deep learning and machine learning models were based on descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation of accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, and precision) across 10-fold cross-validation and a disjoint test set, without inferential statistical testing.

DB1 (ISIC 2019) + DB2 (Open Dataset).description: Combined training and validation dataset. DB1 images with pen marks and colored patches were removed. 10 balanced subsets of 19,932 images were created for 10-fold cross-validation.

The ML approach had an error rate of 7.5% for malignant and 6.1% for benign images in the preprocessing/segmentation step.

DB3 (Pedro Hispano Hospital).description: Disjoint test set used to evaluate generalization capability. Contains 200 images (160 benign, 40 malignant).
DB3 (Pedro Hispano Hospital).notes: The deep learning model outperformed the machine learning model on the disjoint test set, showing better generalization ability. The ML model's lower specificity is attributed to the segmentation processing step affecting feature extraction.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on pre-trained Inception-v3

architecture_details.structure: 11 inception modules, each consisting of pooling layers and convolutional filters

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (trained using MATLAB Deep Learning Toolbox)

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer (implied by standard Inception-v3 usage for classification)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on pre-trained Inception-v3
Ensemble boosted tree classifier with homology segmentation
preprocessing_techniques: Removal of images containing pen marks and colored patches from the ISIC 2019 dataset (DB1) to avoid confounding factors.
Application of persistent homology using the Ripser python library to artificially destruct irrelevant objects and separate the relevant region from background noise.
Application of an isocontour algorithm based on identifying iso-valued contours to obtain skin lesion segmentation.
Recentering and rotation of images and segmentation masks along the longer axis to establish a standardized reference system.
Resizing of images to 299 × 299 × 3 pixels for the deep learning model input.
Data augmentation via random rotations, shifts, and zooms for the deep learning model training and validation sets.
Balancing of the training dataset by creating 10 randomly selected balanced subsets with equal distribution of benign and malignant classes.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to training and validation sets with a rotation range of [-90°, 90°].
Applied to training and validation sets with a pixel range of [10, 10].
Applied to training and validation sets with a scale range of [0.5, 1.5].

To address class imbalance, 10 different balanced datasets were created from the training dataset by randomly selecting only 65% of the benign images from the ISIC 2019 dataset (DB1) to match the number of malignant images, resulting in 10 equally distributed datasets of 19,932 images each.

conclusions: The deep learning-based method (CNN based on pre-trained Inception-v3) outperformed the classical machine learning method (ensemble boosted tree classifier with homology segmentation) in classifying benign vs. malignant dermoscopic skin lesion images, achieving an accuracy of 85.4 ± 3.2% and specificity of 75.5 ± 7.6% on a disjoint test set compared to 73.8 ± 1.1% accuracy and 44.5 ± 4.7% specificity for the ML model.
The deep learning model demonstrated superior generalization ability compared to the machine learning approach when evaluated on a disjoint dataset independent of the training data.

The lower performance of the machine learning model, particularly its lower specificity, is attributed to the preprocessing and segmentation step affecting feature extraction, whereas the deep learning model's architecture avoids increasing the segmentation error rate.

While the deep learning approach showed better classification performance, the machine learning approach offers better explainability and interpretability, which is a critical consideration for clinical applications.

Both AI approaches demonstrated sufficient reliability and robustness to support clinicians in melanoma screening, potentially enabling faster population screening, decreasing diagnostic variability among dermatologists, and facilitating the study of new features between benign and malignant lesions.

The study highlights the importance of using completely disjoint and independent datasets for testing to correctly evaluate the real performance, robustness, and generalization capabilities of AI models in clinical contexts.
important_remarks: The study emphasizes the critical importance of using completely disjoint and independent datasets for testing to avoid bias and correctly evaluate generalization capabilities, noting that many prior studies train and test on the same dataset.

The deep learning model (Inception-v3) significantly outperformed the machine learning model (ensemble boosted tree) on the disjoint test set, achieving 85.4% accuracy and 75.5% specificity compared to 73.8% accuracy and 44.5% specificity for the ML model.

The machine learning model's lower specificity is attributed to errors in the preprocessing and segmentation step (homology segmentation), which affected feature extraction, whereas the deep learning model avoided this issue by processing raw images.

The training dataset was artificially balanced by creating 10 subsets with equal numbers of benign and malignant images, addressing the natural class imbalance present in the source datasets (ISIC 2019 and the open dataset).

The machine learning approach offers better explainability and interpretability compared to the deep learning 'black box,' which is a significant consideration for clinical trust and understanding model behavior.

The disjoint test set (DB3 from Pedro Hispano Hospital) was small (200 images) and lacked demographic metadata (age and sex), limiting the assessment of demographic bias or generalizability across different patient populations.

The study did not perform formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to compare the models; performance differences were assessed using descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation) across 10-fold cross-validation and the test set.

Images with pen marks and colored patches were manually removed from the ISIC 2019 dataset to prevent confounding factors, which may limit the model's robustness to real-world clinical images containing such artifacts.

The authors acknowledge that while both models showed sufficient reliability, large-scale prospective clinical trials are necessary to demonstrate real-world effectiveness, applicability, and safety in early melanoma diagnosis.
